title: The State of Venture Capital
stats: Total venture capital deployed dropped from $330 billion at the 2021 peak to roughly $170 billion by 2024—a decline of nearly fifty percent
The number of deals fell by a similar proportion, from over 17,000 to around 9,500
Perhaps most telling, the number of funds raised collapsed by sixty-four percent, from over 1,100 to approximately 400
Since 2022, LPs have been called for over $400 billion in capital contributions, but they've received only about $200 billion in distributions
takeaways: What does this mean for each player in the ecosystem?  If you're a founder, raise capital when you can, not when you must.
Consider emerging managers seriously; they'll give you more attention and make faster decisions.
Focus on ownership percentage rather than check size.
By 2025, we should see early signs: select IPOs returning, M&A activity picking up, and AI driving a new investment cycle.
By 2026, the IPO window should open wider, LP distributions should increase, and fund formation should rebound.

title: The J-Curve Is Gone (For Now)
stats: Instead, funds spiked to 1.5x or 2x paper value by year two, then flatlined to year five and beyond
In 2021, venture capital deployed $330 billion—roughly double a normal year
Consider a hypothetical $100 million fund raised in 2021
In year one, the manager deployed $30 million across ten companies at an average entry valuation of $20 million
takeaways: Consider a hypothetical $100 million fund raised in 2021.
LPs are starting to ask: "When do we see real returns?"  The gap between expectations and reality is substantial.
What should different players take from this?  Limited Partners shouldn't panic-sell on secondary markets at forty percent or greater discounts.
General Partners must be honest about marks.
Focus on helping portfolio companies reach profitability, which gives them control over their own destiny.

title: Why Vertical AI Wins
stats: That game belongs to Big Tech companies with their $50 billion-plus annual R&D budgets
More than 140 vertical AI startups have reached significant scale, with a combined valuation exceeding $120 billion
Healthcare and biotech lead with eighteen companies worth $24.5 billion combined
Financial services follows with fifteen companies worth $19.2 billion
takeaways: The biggest opportunity in artificial intelligence isn't building foundation models.
Founders should pick one vertical and go absurdly deep—you can't out-generalize OpenAI.
Investors should score every vertical AI deal against this framework and always ask "What data do you have that OpenAI doesn't?" Enterprises should buy vertical AI where it exists.

title: Enterprise AI Adoption
takeaways: Individual contributors say "Why should I train my replacement?" What they want is job security and fair compensation.
Enterprises can assess their AI readiness across six factors.
The build versus buy decision should usually favor buying.
Without that proprietary data, you should still buy—you can't replicate what specialized vendors have accumulated.
The reality is that ninety percent or more of enterprises should buy AI solutions rather than building them.

title: VC Fund Economics
stats: Consider a typical $100 million fund
The structure includes a two percent annual management fee over ten years, totaling $20 million
Carried interest is twenty percent of profits above a 1x return
This means that of the $100 million committed by LPs, only $80 million is actually investable after management fees
takeaways: Consider a typical $100 million fund.
The fund must generate $360 million in gross returns.
That leaves just twenty percent as potential winners, and they must generate the bulk of returns.
The key metrics in venture capital are TVPI, DPI, RVPI, and IRR.
By years three and four, TVPI should reach 1.1x to 1.5x with DPI between zero and 0.2x.

title: Emerging Managers
stats: Emerging managers—those managing Funds I through III, typically under $100 million—consistently outperform established funds at early-stage investing
A $50 million fund with a $200 million exit gets a four-times contribution from that single deal
A $1 billion fund with the same $200 million exit barely moves the needle
In a single recent quarter, Andreessen Horowitz raised $7.2 billion—capturing roughly eighty percent of all venture LP commitments
takeaways: Founders lose capital for the earliest stages, investors who actually have time to help, diversity of funding options, and leverage in negotiations.
Emerging GPs should focus relentlessly on Fund I track record; everything else is noise.
Founders should take meetings with emerging managers; they're hungrier and more available.
Everyone should recognize that a healthy ecosystem needs diverse capital sources.

title: Family Offices
stats: Family offices write checks ranging from $100,000 to $10 million based on relationship and interest; VCs deploy according to defined fund strategy
They invest in a hands-on, angel-like style with typical checks of $250,000 to $2 million
Checks range from $1 million to $10 million if they participate at all
Checks range from $2 million to $20 million
takeaways: Always start with relationship building.
GPs should build family office relationships years before you need them and remember that every family office is different—understand their unique situation.
Founders should consider family offices as strategic angels since they often have industry expertise and patient capital.
Everyone should recognize that family offices are the backbone of early-stage venture, even though they rarely get credit for it.

title: DPI Pressure Is Destroying Value
stats: Expected DPI by year five went from 0.3x to 0.5x
Consider a company that could exit for $500 million in seven years or $200 million in four years
The seven-year exit generates $50 million at ten percent ownership, contributing 0.5x DPI to a $100 million fund
The four-year exit generates $20 million, contributing 0.2x DPI
takeaways: LPs understandably want their money back, but the timing and structure of these demands is counterproductive.
Consider a company that could exit for $500 million in seven years or $200 million in four years.
LPs expected quick returns based on fast rounds.
Don't raise from LPs who will pressure you into bad decisions.

title: VC Is in the Exit Business
stats: Required scale is usually $30 million or more in ARR for meaningful acquisitions
Companies typically need $200 million or more in revenue with strong growth
If your fund needs $500 million in exits to generate target returns, map out how you get there
takeaways: LPs commit capital with the expectation of receiving it back, plus returns.
GPs receive carry only when profits are realized.
Founders get liquidity only through exits.
Investors evaluate opportunities based on team quality, market size, product differentiation, and competitive dynamics.
But they frequently don't ask the fundamental question: who will buy this company, and at what price?  Every investment decision should start with exit analysis.

title: The IPO Pipeline Is Broken
stats: The median revenue at IPO went from $20 million to over $200 million
Total venture-backed IPOs fell from over 300 annually to fewer than fifty
Companies with $50 billion private valuations can't easily go public—the market can't absorb them
takeaways: Companies with clear paths to sustainable free cash flow.
For GPs and founders, the implications are significant.
Build companies that can go public—that means focusing on profitability and durable unit economics, not just growth.
Develop relationships with potential acquirers as a backup path.
Consider employee liquidity programs to retain talent during extended private periods.

title: M&A Is Dead—Long Live Hire and License
takeaways: Negotiate your employment package carefully—that may be where the real value lies.
For investors, adjust your exit expectations.
Traditional M&A may not be available for some portfolio companies.
Evaluate companies on their ability to reach profitability and independence.
Build relationships with potential acquirers early to understand their preferred transaction structures.

title: Secondaries Are Reshaping Liquidity
stats: Transaction volume reached $150 billion in 2023, up from $30 billion a decade earlier
takeaways: But understand pricing—secondary buyers are sophisticated and won't pay more than they need to.
Tax implications can be significant for large sales.
Secondaries are not a replacement for traditional exits, but they've become an essential part of the liquidity landscape.
Understanding how they work is important for everyone in the venture ecosystem.

---

# PART V: BIG TECH AND INFRASTRUCTURE

---.

title: Build vs. Buy—Why Enterprises Must Buy
stats: These people command $500,000 to $1 million or more in total compensation
takeaways: Does AI provide competitive differentiation in your core product? If no, buy.
If yes, is AI your core business? If no, buy.
If yes, do you have proprietary data that creates genuine advantage? If no, buy.
The reality is that ninety percent or more of enterprises should buy AI solutions.
Building makes sense only for companies where AI is the core product and they have both the proprietary data and the organizational capability to execute.

title: The Billion-Dollar Engineer
stats: When Inflection AI essentially sold its team to Microsoft for around $650 million, the per-engineer price approached $100 million
Microsoft has a market capitalization exceeding $3 trillion
One percent of $3 trillion is $30 billion
Against that scale, paying $650 million for a world-class team is a rounding error
takeaways: Building an exceptional team is valuable, but the value may accrue through hiring transactions rather than equity.
Negotiate carefully to ensure value flows to the right places.
For investors, evaluate teams on their acquisition potential.
But recognize that acquirers are increasingly sophisticated about structuring transactions to minimize what flows to shareholders.

---

# PART VI: PRACTICAL FRAMEWORKS

---.

title: Fundraising by Stage
stats: At the pre-seed stage, you're typically raising $500,000 to $2 million to prove that the founding team can build something
Valuations range from $5 million to $15 million pre-money
At seed stage, you're raising $2 million to $5 million to prove initial product-market fit
Valuations range from $10 million to $25 million pre-money
takeaways: Investors are betting on people, not metrics.
Investors want to see early traction: users, engagement, initial revenue.
Investors are seed funds, some multi-stage funds, and angels investing larger amounts.
Investors need to see meaningful traction: typically $1 million to $3 million in ARR with strong growth.
Investors are Series A specialists and multi-stage funds.

title: The Startup Fundamentals
takeaways: Before chasing AI trends or market timing, founders must nail the fundamentals.
The math of acquiring and serving customers must be profitable.
Customer acquisition cost divided by customer lifetime value must yield a healthy ratio.
Payback periods should typically be eighteen months or less.

title: Valuation Benchmarks
stats: For pre-seed companies with a team and idea but no product, valuations typically range from $3 million to $8 million pre-money
For seed companies with a product and initial users but limited revenue, valuations range from $8 million to $20 million pre-money
For Series A companies, which should have $1 million to $3 million in ARR with strong growth, valuations range from $30 million to $80 million pre-money, representing roughly ten to thirty times ARR
For Series B companies with $5 million to $15 million in ARR and demonstrated unit economics, valuations range from $80 million to $250 million pre-money, roughly eight to twenty times ARR
takeaways: These ranges vary by sector, growth rate, team quality, and market conditions.
Companies with slower growth trade at discounts.
The most important valuation advice: don't optimize for it.
A higher valuation with misaligned investors is worse than a lower valuation with great partners.
Valuation matters, but it's not the only thing that matters.

title: What Happens Next
takeaways: The great companies of the next decade are being built now.
Economic downturns clear out weak competitors and force discipline on survivors.
Founders building during difficult times develop skills and cultures that serve them well.
The next generation of category-defining companies is emerging from this period.
The best opportunities emerge when others are retreating.

title: LP Due Diligence on Fund Managers
takeaways: The best LPs go beyond track record to understand what drives performance.
Raw returns matter, but attribution matters more.
They understand that a single fund is insufficient data for assessment.
They evaluate process and judgment, not just outcomes.
They recognize that the best managers often have unconventional approaches.

title: Founder Assessment
stats: Exceptional companies with $3 million or more in ARR and demonstrated unit economics can reach $80-100 million
Greater than 120% is excellent; below 100% indicates net churn
For software, should exceed 70%
Should exceed 85% for SMB, 90% or more for enterprise
takeaways: Investors evaluating founders need frameworks that go beyond pattern matching.
The most important top-line metric for SaaS companies.
For software, should exceed 70%.
Should typically be under eighteen months for efficient businesses.
Should exceed 3:1 for healthy economics.
